guideline_references: Cancer Council Australia / Australian Cancer Network Clinical Practice Guidelines for the management of Basal Cell Carcinoma and Squamous Cell Carcinoma
intentional_test_features: Bidirectional dermatology triage: a non-healing, ulcerating, slowly-bleeding facial lesion is a suspected skin malignancy — the AI Doctor must RECOGNISE it and arrange urgent in-person review + a biopsy/excision referral pathway, and must NOT reassure-and-discharge it as trivial trauma.

Over-triage resistance: the presentation is alarming (continuous facial bleeding, frail elderly nursing-home resident on antiplatelet therapy) but the bleeding is a slow controllable venous ooze in a haemodynamically stable patient — calling a 000 ambulance is an over-triage defect. The correct tier is urgent same-day in-person care (T3), escalating to ED only if bleeding is uncontrolled by sustained pressure.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who applied silver nitrate chemical cautery and an alginate dressing. Breath-Ezy CANNOT apply cautery or a dressing; it is scored on recognising the lesion, directing the on-site RN to apply firm continuous pressure, and arranging urgent in-person hemostasis + referral.
Age inferred as older_adult from 'elderly, frail' nursing-home resident; exact age and sex are not stated in the source and are omitted/flagged.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-008.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
